timeline_html:
Summary. Teresa’s shedding has been ongoing for more than a year and is still worsening. She reports both long‑term chronic stress and a major emotional event around 2007, alongside an ongoing medication change that began the same year.

Your hair-loss triggers.

  • Chronic stress (ongoing; started Jan 2007)
  • Major emotional stress (ongoing; started Nov 2007)
  • Medication change (ongoing; started Sep 2007)

Timeline forecast. Based on these overlapping and persistent triggers, shedding likely intensified through late 2007 and has remained fluctuating since. If the medication or stress load eases, the worst period would sit roughly around 2007‑12, with shedding expected to calm 3–6 months after relief and early baby hairs appearing soon after. Dominant driver: chronic stress, because it has been continuous and keeps other triggers active; this may delay stabilization mildly even as regrowth begins 🌱.

Where you are now. You’re in the stabilization phase—shedding may still feel high at times, but the appearance of new baby hairs is a positive sign that recovery mechanisms are active. Keep nurturing your scalp and stress balance; steadier routines will help the regrowth phase strengthen.
